Facts.text: August 13, 3114 BC, is the start of the Maya calendar according to the Lounsbury correlation
St John I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Raniero elected as Pope Paschal II
Neopagan religious figure Aradia de Toscano initiated into a Dianic witchcraft cult; goes on to found tradition of Stregheria (according to Wicca writer Raven Grimassi)
The 7th Portuguese Armada to India arrives at Mombasa Island, a major Arab trading port on the Swahili coast, and proceeds to sack and plunder the city into submission
The Treaty of Noyon between France and Spain is signed. Francis recognizes Charles's claim to Naples, and Charles recognizes Francis's claim to Milan
Buddhist monks from Kyoto's Enryaku-ji Temple set fire to 21 Nichiren temples throughout Kyoto in the Tenbun Hokke Disturbance (Traditional Japanese date: July 27, 1536)
Duke Frans of Anjou recognized as protector of Netherlands
The Second Treaty of Brömsebro is signed between Sweden and Denmark-Norway, ending the Torstenson War
Hunger strike in Rotterdam
British parliament accepts East India Company Act 1784, bringing the East India Company's rule in India under the control of the British Government.
Prussia joins the Anglo-Dutch alliance to form the Triple Alliance to prevent the spread of the Russo-Swedish War of 1788-90
British fleet under Lord Seymour overthrows Suriname
Anglo-Dutch Treaty: Cape of Good Hope is formally ceded to the British, and the transportation of slaves is prohibited
Steamer "Atlantic" crossing Lake Erie from Buffalo to Detroit collides with a fishing boat and sinks with 250 aboard
Battle of Deep Bottom, Virginia (Strawberry Plains) and Fussell's Mill, Virginia
Earthquake kills 25,000 and causes $300 million of damage in Peru and Ecuador
William Gray of Hartford, Connecticut patents the coin-operated telephone
US black newspaper "Afro-American" begins publishing from Baltimore
English cricket team beats Australia by one wicket in a famous victory at The Oval in London
Chicago Cubs pitcher Jack Taylor ends a streak of completing 202 MLB games (187 complete, 15 relief) against the Brooklyn Superbas in the third inning of an 11-3 win at Washington Park, NYC
First taxicabs, imported by Harry N. Allen, operate in New York City

Brooklyn Superbas and Pittsburgh Pirates play "the game of perfect symmetry" in baseball to an 8-8 tie, both have 8 runs, 13 hits, 2 errors, 12 assists, 5 strikeouts, 3 walks, 1 hit batsman, and 1 passed ball
Invention of stainless steel by Harry Brearley of Sheffield, England
Carl Wickman begins Greyhound, the first US bus line, in Minnesota
German army occupies forts in Liege, Belgium
A revolt in Catalonia, the province in northeast Spain that has long seen itself as independent
Milt Stock (2), Fred Luderus (2), and Possum Whitted of the Philadelphia Phillies steal five bases in an inning in a 3-0 win against Boston at Braves Field
Bayerische Motoren Werke AG (BMW) is established as a public company in Germany
American thoroughbred racehorse Man o' War's only defeat in a 21-start career; Upset wins the Sanford Memorial Stakes at Saratoga Race Course
US Steel Corp initiates an 8-hour workday
Enrique Tirabocchi of Argentina becomes the first to swim the English Channel from France to England (record 16 hours 33 minutes)
Soviet Union Spartakiad Games begins
Cincinnati Reds second baseman Tony Cuccinello goes 6 for 6 in 17-3 rout of Boston at Braves Field
Jacques van Egmond becomes world champion amateur cyclist
Roller derby is born when the Transcontinental Roller Derby begins at Chicago Coliseum
Americans Dorothy Poynton-Hill and Velma Dunn take the gold and silver medals in the 10 m platform diving at the Berlin Olympics
Nida Senff and Rie Mastenbroek make it a Dutch 1-2 in the women’s 100 m backstroke final at the Berlin Olympics
Rie Mastenbroek of the Netherlands swims an Olympic record of 1:05.9 to win the women's 100 m freestyle at the Berlin Olympics, beating Jeannette Campbell of Argentina by 0.5 seconds
Second Sino-Japanese War: Japanese forces start the Battle of Shanghai, a three-month conflict involving over one million troops
Yankees set AL shutout margin record with a 21-0 victory over the A's
The Red Army evacuates Smolensk, Russia, as the German Army advances
The Red army recaptures Spas-Demensk from the German army in Kaluga Oblast, Russia
British 8th Army occupies Florence
Britain diverts illegal immigrants bound for Palestine to Cyprus
Gunnar Gren scores twice as Sweden beats Yugoslavia 3-1 to win the football gold medal at the London Olympics
Hungarian boxer László Papp wins the first of three consecutive Olympic gold medals, defeating British middleweight Johnny Wright on points at the London Games
José Beyaert of France wins the cycling road race at the London Olympics; Belgium takes gold in the team section, beating Great Britain and France
The United States successfully defends its Olympic basketball title with a 65-21 win over France at the London Games
Three Finnish gymnasts tie for the gold medal in the pommel horse section at the London Olympics; Paavo Aaltonen, Veikko Huhtanen, and Heikki Savolainen are all awarded gold
Great Britain and Iraq sign a new oil contract

Blues singer Willie Mae "Big Mama" Thornton records the song "Hound Dog," written a day earlier especially for her by Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller at Radio Recorders Studio in Los Angeles, California
4-5 million French people go on strike against austerity measures
21st NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Detroit 31, All-Stars 6 (93,470 attendees)
WBIR TV channel 10 in Knoxville, TN (CBS) begins broadcasting
US Military satellite Discoverer 5 launches into polar orbit
Central African Republic proclaims independence from France
USSR withdraws advisors from China
Construction of the Berlin Wall begins in East Germany
Dave DeBusscherepitches a shutout against the Cleveland Indians
US Customs agents confiscate 21 gold coins from Witte Museum, San Antonio, Texas
Warren Spahnsets the left-hander strikeout record at 2,382
First broadcast by Trans World Radio on Bonaire
Longview, Texas, radio station KLUE is struck by lightning the day after holding a "Beatles Bonfire," knocking an engineer unconscious and causing extensive damage to equipment
WQLN TV channel 54 in Erie, Pennsylvania (PBS) begins broadcasting
Baltimore OrioleJim Palmerno-hits the Oakland A's, 8-0
Pamphlet bombs that scatter revolutionary African National Congress propaganda leaflets explode in Cape Town, Johannesburg, Durban, and Port Elizabeth, South Africa
Dutch KRO-TV transmits the 440th and last episode of the American western series "Bonanza"

Bayardo Bar attack: Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers carry out a gun and bomb attack on a pub in Belfast frequented by Ulster Volunteer Force commanders; 4 Protestant civilians and 1 UVF member are killed
South Africa pledges support for a negotiated settlement in Rhodesia
Canadian guitarist Randy Bachman quits the band Bachman-Turner Overdrive, and they then disband
First test glide of Space Shuttle
Bomb attack in Beirut kills 175
MLB St. Louis Cardinals'Lou Brockbecomes the 14th MLB player to achieve 3,000 hits
Surinamese President Johan Ferrier is ousted
Tatyana Kazankina of the USSR sets a world record in the women's 1,500 meter with a time of 3:52.47 in Moscow, USSR
American swimmer Mary T. Meagher breaks her own world women's 200 m butterfly record with a time of 2:05.96 in Brown Deer, Wisconsin
Last broadcast of "The Waltons" on CBS-TV
Test cricket debut of Mike Whitney for Australia against England at Old Trafford
Morocco and Libya sign the Arabic-African Union treaty

South African Defence Force soldiers, traveling in a convoy of more than ten armored vehicles, surround the Thaba-Jabula High School in Soweto and arrest 200 students during a clampdown on school boycotts
KRE-AM in Berkeley, CA, changes call letters to KBLX (now KBFN)
Cardinals outfield sets record of no putouts in a 4-2 win in 13 innings
Boston Red Sox win an AL record of 24 consecutive home games
Palace of Auburn Hills in Detroit opens
Ronald J. Dossenbach sets a world record for pedaling across Canada from Vancouver, BC, to Halifax, NS, in 13 days, 15 hours, and 4 minutes
US beats Jamaica 5-1 in the second round of the 1990 World Cup qualifiers
Two hot-air balloons crash at Alice Springs, Australia, killing 13 people
US space shuttle STS-28 lands
American Michael Ray Barrowman swims a world record in the 200 m breaststroke (2:10.60) at the World Championships in Fort Lauderdale, Florida
Ruffhouse/Columbia Records releases "Cypress Hill", the debut studio album by American hip-hop music group Cypress Hill; it achieves double-platinum status with over 2 million units sold
US Vice-President Dan Quayle makes a speech attacking lawyers at the annual meeting of the American Bar Association
Hotel in Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand, collapses, 114 killed
US Court of Appeals rules Congress must save all emails
Train crash in Tbilisi, Georgia, kills 24 people
Microsoft releases Internet Explorer 3.0
Boston Red Sox trade Mike Stanley back to the NY Yankees
MLB San Diego Padres trade outfielderRickey Hendersonto Anaheim Angels
156 Congolese Tutsi refugees massacred at the Gatumba refugee camp in Burundi
28th Olympic Games open in Athens, Greece
Black Friday crackdown by the Maldivian National Security Service on a peaceful protest in the capital city of Malé, Maldives
Hurricane Charley, a Category 4 storm, strikes Punta Gorda, Florida, and devastates the surrounding area
Cleveland Indians first baseman Travis Hafner ties Don Mattingly's MLB record of six grand slams in a season when he homers off Kansas City's Luke Hudson
Australian swimmer Stephanie Rice wraps up the medley double at the Beijing Olympics as she swims a world record of 2:08.45 to win the 200 m individual medley gold medal

Italian swimmer Federica Pellegrini sets a new world record of 1:54.82 to claim the 200 m freestyle gold medal at the Beijing Olympics; runner-up Sara Isaković becomes Slovenia's first Olympic swim medal winner
Nadzeya Ostapchuk of Belarus is stripped of her shot-put gold medal after failing a drug test
Iranian mathematician Maryam Mirzakhani is the first woman to win the Fields Medal in mathematics
The Israeli and Palestinian ceasefire agreed to on August 8 is extended for another 120 hours to provide time for further negotiations
76 people are killed and 212 are wounded by an ISIL truck bomb in Baghdad, Iraq
American swimmerMichael Phelpsends his career at the Rio Olympics as part of the winning 4 x 100 m medley relay, his record 23rd gold medal
Great Britain sets a world record of 3:14.051 to comfortably beat the United States in the women's cycling team pursuit at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
New York Yankees rookies Tyler Austin andAaron Judgehit back-to-back home runs in their first Major League plate appearances in an 8-4 win over the Tampa Bay Rays at Yankee Stadium, NYC
The dominant Great Britain men's crew wins the eight at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; US scores a comfortable win over GB in the women's eight
Gunmen kill at least 18 at a café in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso; Islamic extremists are blamed
More than 60 children are reported to have died at Gorakhpur Hospital, India, prompting an investigation
Entertainment companies CBS and Viacom announce they will merge with a combined $28 billion in revenue
Measles cases triple worldwide in 2019 according to WHO, with 364,808 reported compared to 129,239 in 2018
Israel strikes a historic deal with the United Arab Emirates to normalize relations, and Israel suspends plans to annex parts of the occupied West Bank

New details about the enormous "Terror Crocodile" (Deinosuchus), the size of a bus with teeth as big as bananas, that lived during the late Cretaceous period (75-84 million years ago) in North America are published
Suspected airstrike on Finote Selam in Ethiopia's Amhara region kills at least 26 people as government forces fight the Fano paramilitary force

Sean Dunn (37) is charged with felony assault three days after hurling expletives, invectives, and a submarine-sandwich at a Customs and Border Protection agent patrolling the streets of Washington, D.C.
Spanish conquistadors led byHernán Cortéscapture Aztec EmperorCuauhtémocin Tenochtitlan, bringing the Aztec Empire to an end
Spanish theologian and physician Michael Servetus is arrested as a heretic in Geneva, put on trial the next day, eventually found guilty of heresy, and burned to death

Controversial play "Eastward Hoe" byBen Jonson, George Chapman, and John Marston premieres at the Blackfriars Theatre, London, landing two of the authors in prison for offending the King James I [date approximate]
John Smith's story of Jamestown's first days submitted for publication
Cardinal Richelieu is appointed Chief Minister of France byLouis XIII
Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIdismisses his supreme commanderAlbrecht von Wallenstein
Dutch astronomerChristiaan Huygensdiscovers Mars's southern polar cap
Bombardment of Brussels begins by the French army ofLouis XIV; the Grand-Place and much of the city are destroyed during the Nine Years' War
Battle of Blenheim (Second Battle of Höchstädt): A combined British, German and Dutch army led byJohn Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough, decisively beats the French and Bavarians
Voltaire's tragic play "Zaire" premieres in Paris
Revolutionaries imprison French royals, includingMarie Antoinette
The Bayreuth Festspielhaus opens with the first complete performance ofRichard Wagner's Ring Cycle
John A. Macdonald, Canada's first Prime Minister, uses a silver hammer to drive a gold spike, officially completing the Esquimalt and Nanaimo Railway on Vancouver Island in British Columbia
US forces under AdmiralGeorge Deweycapture Manila during Spanish–American War

Black soldiers are accused of raiding Brownsville in Texas; despite support from local commanding officers, PresidentTheodore Rooseveltorders dishonorable discharge for 167 soldiers; all are cleared of wrongdoing in 1972, 165 posthumously
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherCy Young's career is celebrated with a special day in his name in Boston as he pitches briefly for the Red Sox against an All-Star team
KingEdward VIIof Great Britain meets Emperor Franz Joseph of Austria at Ischl; the King tries to persuade the Emperor to advise Germany against aggressive anti-British policies
Flag designed byMarcus Garvey, consisting of three horizontal stripes of red, black, and green, is adopted as the Pan-African flag
Simon Kaufman andMarc Connelly's comedic play "Dulcy" premieres in New York City
Gustav Stresemannbecomes the premier of the German coalition government
Turkish National Congress selectsMustafa Kemal Atatürkas the first President of Turkey
Adolf Hitlerrefuses President von Hindenburg's proposal to become Vice-Chancellor of Germany
Yankees pitcherRed Ruffingbecomes the first MLB pitcher since 1906 to hit an extra-inning, game-winning home run in New York's 1-0 win over the Senators in Washington
Allied generalsBernard Montgomery,Miles DempseyandOmar Bradleydiscuss a future breakthrough in the European theatre during World War II
Jackie GleasonLes Tremayne show premieres on NBC radio
Satchel Paige, at 42, pitches his first major league complete game as Cleveland defeats the White Sox 5-0 in Chicago
US PresidentHarry Trumangives military aid to Vietnamese regime ofBảo Đại
US PresidentDwight Eisenhowerestablishes Government Contract Compliance Committee
Larry Dobyends the American League record of 167 errorless games in the outfield
Elvis Presleyreleases the music single "Don't Be Cruel"
MLB Cleveland Indians' right fielderRocky Colavitomakes his pitching debut, hurling three hitless innings, Detroit 3, Cleveland 2
Bert Campanerisof Daytona Beach (FSL) pitches with both arms and plays all nine positions
"Bonnie and Clyde" crime film directed by Arthur Penn and starringWarren BeattyandFaye Dunawayis released
Temporary CommissionerBowie Kuhnis elected for a 7-year term as MLB Commissioner by a unanimous vote
Paul andLinda McCartneyrelease the single "Back Seat of My Car"
Jackie Joyner-Kerseeties the world record long jump with a 24 feet 5½ inches (7.46 m) jump

American soul singerCurtis Mayfieldbecomes paralyzed from the neck down after stage lighting equipment falls on him at an outdoor concert at Wingate Field in Flatbush, Brooklyn, New York; although unable to perform live, he continues to compose, sing, and record
MLB Toronto Blue Jays outfielderRickey Hendersonpays Turner Ward $25,000 for his uniform number 24
Animated TV sitcom "South Park" debuts on Comedy Central

American super-swimmerMichael Phelpswins three gold medals, all in world record time, in one day at the Beijing Olympics: 200 m IM (1:54.23), 200 m butterfly (1:52.03), and 4x200m freestyle relay (6:58.56)
"Crash My Party," the fourth studio album byLuke Bryan, is released (Billboard Album of the Year 2014)
Swedish prosecutors announce they are dropping allegations againstWikiLeaksfounderJulian Assangeof sexual molestation and coercion
The US government formally returnsPicasso's painting La Coiffeuse to France after it was stolen from Paris' National Museum of Modern Art in 2001

British superstarMo Farahwins the first leg of the 5,000/10,000 m double (a repeat of London 2012) in 27:05.17 in the 10 km at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; Kenya's Paul Tanui wins silver just 0.47 seconds behind

Jamaican sprinter Elaine Thompson wins the women's 100 m gold in 10.71 at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; American Tori Bowie finishes second in 10.83, andShelly-Ann Fraser-Pryceof Jamaica finishes third in 10.86
Grace Mugabe, wife of the President of Zimbabwe,Robert Mugabe, is accused of assault in Johannesburg, South Africa
Ugandan politician and pop singerBobi Wineis arrested by the Ugandan army at a campaign rally in Arua and is later allegedly tortured
Otto Witte, German acrobat and fantasist, is purportedly crowned King of Albania
Battle of Britain:Hermann Goering's "Adlertag" (Eagle Day) offensive takes place, intending to destroy the Royal Air Force; 47-48 German aircraft are shot down, and the RAF loses 25 planes
The Manhattan Project commences under the direction of US GeneralLeslie Groveswith the aim of developing an atomic bomb
Arnulf of Metz, French bishop and saint, born in Lay-Saint-Christophe, France (d. 640)
Alfonso XI, King of Castile and León (1312-50), born in Salamanca. Spain (d. 1350)
Theophilus Howard, 2nd Earl of Suffolk, English politician, born in Saffron Waldon, Essex, England (d. 1640)
Johannes Andreas Quenstedt, German Lutheran theologian, born in Quedlinburg, Germany (d. 1688)
Rasmus Bartholin, Danish physician, mathematician and physicist, born in Roskilde, Denmark (d. 1698)
Johann Christoph Denner, German musician and inventor (clarinet), baptized in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1707)
Charles Seymour, 6th Duke of Somerset, English politician, born in Wiltshire, England (d. 1748)
William Wotton, English theologian and scholar, born in Wrentham, Suffolk (d. 1727)
Anton Simon Ignaz Praelisauer, German organist and composer, baptized in Kötzting, Electorate of Bavaria (d. 1746)
John Dyer, Welsh painter and poet, (Grongar Hill), born in Aberglasney, Wales (d. 1758) (Baptism date)
Heinrich von Brühl, German statesman, born in Gangloffsömmern, Thuringia, Germany (d. 1763)
Lorenzo Fago, Italian composer and teacher, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1793)
Christoph Nichelmann, German composer and harpsichordist, born in Treuenbrietzen, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1762)
Louis François I, Prince of Conti, French nobleman and military leader, born in Paris (d. 1776)
Francis Hutcheson, Irish composer, born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1784)
Adrien-Joseph van Helmont, Flemish composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1830)
Pieter L. van de Kasteele, Dutch politician and patriot, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1810)
Maria Carolina of Austria, Queen of the Two Sicilies, born in Schönbrunn Palace, Vienna (d. 1814)
Louis Baraguey d'Hilliers, French general, born in Paris (d. 1813)
George Bridgetower, Polish-African violinist (musical prodigy and friend of Beethoven), born in Eastern Poland (d. 1860)[1]
William Wentworth, Australian explorer and politician, born in Norfolk Island (d. 1872)
Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen, Queen consort of William IV of the United Kingdom, born in Meiningen, Germany (d. 1849)
Nikolaus Lenau, Hungarian-German poet (Faust, Die Albigenser), born in Lenauheim, Banat, Romania (d. 1850)
Vladimir Odoevsky, Russian philosopher and writer, born in Moscow (d. 1869)
Anders Jonas Ångström, Swedish physicist (Spectroscopy), born in Lögdö, Medelpad, Sweden (d. 1874)
Károly Thern, Hungarian composer, born in Spišská Nová Ves, Slovakia (d. 1886)
Lucy Stone, American abolitionist (US Woman's Suffrage Association), born in West Brookefield, Massachusetts (d. 1893)

George Grove, British civil engineer, writer, musicologist, music historian (Grove's Dictionary of Music and Musicians), and administrator (Director of Royal College of Music, 1882-94), born in Clapham, London (d. 1900)
Joseph Alberdingk Thijm, Dutch poet (Dietsche Warande), born in Amsterdam (d. 1889)
Goldwin Smith, English historian and journalist, born in Reading, Berkshire (d. 1910)
Rodolphe Bresdin, French visionary engraver and lithographer (Le Bon Samaritain), born in Le Fresne-sur-Loire, France (d. 1885)
William Thomas Best, English organist and composer, born in Carlisle, Cumberland, England (d. 1897)
Salomon Jadassohn, German pianist and composer, born in Wrocław, Poland (d. 1902)
St. Nikolai [Kasatkin] of Japan, Russian-born Eastern Orthodox priest who introduced the Eastern Orthodox Church to Japan, born in Smolensk, Russian Empire (d. 1912)
Johnny Mullagh, Australian cricket all-rounder (Aboriginal tour of England 1868), born in Harrow, Australia (d. 1891)
Felix Adler, German-American educator of political and social ethics, born in Alzey, Grand Duchy of Hesse, Germany (d. 1933)
John Lincoln Clem, American general and drummer boy (Union Army), born in Newark, Ohio (d. 1937)
Giovanni Agnelli, Italian businessman and founder of Fiat (Fabbrica Italiana di Automobili Torino) Automaker, born in Villar Perosa, Italy (d. 1945)
Rudolf Georg Binding, German songwriter and writer (Legenden Der Zeit), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1938)
William Alexander Craigie, Scottish lexicographer (Skotlands Rimur), born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1957)
Karl Liebknecht, German Marxist revolutionary (Sparta Party), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1919)
Frank Mitchell, English cricket batsman (5 Tests, HS 41; Yorkshire CCC, MCC) and rugby union forward (6 Tests; Blackheath RFC), born in Market Weighton, England (d. 1935)
Richard Willstätter, German chemist (Nobel 1915), born in Karlsruhe, Baden (d. 1942)
Leonid Nikolayev, Russian-Soviet pianist and composer, born in Kyiv, Ukraine (d. 1942)
Salomon "Sam" de Wolff, Dutch zionist and socialist theorist, born in Sneek, Netherlands (d. 1960)
John Ireland, English composer and pianist (Mai-Dun), born in Bowdon, Cheshire, England (d. 1962)
Jacqueline Reyneke van Stuwe, Dutch author, born in Muntok, Indonesia (d. 1955)

C. Howard Crane, American architect (Fox Theatres; Detroit Olympia Stadium; Detroit Opera House; LeVeque Tower (Columbus, Ohio); Earls Court Exhibition Centre (London, England)), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1952)
Jacobus Hendrik Pierneef, South African painter, born in Pretoria, South Africa (d. 1957)
Julius Freed [Fried], American namesake and co-founder of Orange Julius fast food chain, born in Montana (d. 1952)
Gleb W. Derujinsky, Russian-American sculptor, born in Smolensk, Russia (d. 1975)
Camillien Houde, French Canadian politician and Mayor of Montreal, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1958)
[Lavinia] Marian Fleming Poe, African American advocate in Virginia, born in Warwick, County, Virginia (d. 1974)
Ferdinand Sassen, Dutch philosopher and director, born in Lugano, Switzerland (d. 1971)
Leonid Polovinkin, Russian conductor, and composer (Night Dreams), born in Kurgan, Russian Empire (d. 1949)
Robert Burton, American actor (The Big Heat, Compulsion, A Man Called Peter), born in Eastman, Georgia (d. 1962)
Leo Pappenheim, Dutch conductor, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Regis Toomey, American actor (Burke's Law, Petticoat Junction), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
Gordon Sparling, Canadian pioneering filmmaker (Canadian Cameo series - Canada's first major films with sound), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1994)[1]
Ian Whyte, Scottish composer and conductor (BBC Scottish Symphony Orchestra), born in Dunfermline, Scotland (d. 1960)
Alan Sainsbury, Baron Sainsbury, British business executive, born in London (d. 1998)
Bartoes [Albertus J. Wijstma], Dutch singer and cabaret performer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Guy Paquinet, French jazz trombonist, and bandleader, born in Tours, France (d. 1981)
Wilton Graff, American actor (Bloodlust, Just Before Dawn), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1969)
Charles "Buddy" Rogers, American musician and actor (An Innocent Affair, Wings), born in Olathe, Kansas (d. 1999)

Humphrey Waldock, British lawyer (UN International Law Commission, 1961-72), and jurist (European Court of Human Rights, 1966-74; International Court of Justice, 1973-81), born in Colombo, Ceylon (d. 1981)
Alfried Krupp [né von Bohlen und Halbach], German arms manufacturer, convicted war criminal, and Olympic yacht competitor (1936), born in Essen, Germany (d. 1967)
Basil Spence, British architect, born in Bombay, India (d. 1976)
Gene Raymond [Raymond Guion], American actor (Paris 7000, Love on a Bet, Fireside Theater), born in New York City (d. 1998)
Dave Willock, American actor (Queen of Outer Space), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1990)
John Beal [James Andrew Bliedung], American actor (10 Who Dare, I Am the Law, Les Misérables), born in Joplin, Missouri (d. 1997)
Wilhelmina Von Bremen, American 4X100 relay sprinter (Olympic gold 1932), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1976)
James Benton Parson, American judge, 1st African American federal judge, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1993)
William Bernbach, American advertising creative director, born in New York City (d. 1982)
Max Croiset, Dutch actor (The Village on the River, Dog of Flanders, Little Ark), born in Blaricum, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Metten Koornstra, Dutch painter and graphic artist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1978)
Rita Johnson, American actress (Honolulu, All Mine to Give), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1965)
Salvador Luria, Italian-American microbiologist (Nobel Prize for medicine 1969 genetic structure of viruses), born in Turin, Italy (d. 1991)
“Big Chief” Russell Moore, Pima-American jazz trombonist (Lionel Hampton; Louis Armstrong), and composer ("Wounded Knee Chant"), born in the Gila River Indian Community, Arizona (d. 1983)
Albert Oram, Baron Oram, British Labour politician, born in Winchester, England (d. 1999)
Anatoly Bogatyrev, Belarusian composer, born in Vitebsk, Belarus (d. 2003)

Anna Mae Winburn (née Darden), African American jazz singer and bandleader of all-female, racially integrated big band (International Sweethearts of Rhythm), born in Port Royal, Tennessee (d. 1999)[1][2]
Francisco Escudero, Basque composer (Illetta), born in San Sebastián, Basque Autonomous Community, Spain (d. 2002)
John Facenda, American sportscaster (NFL Action), born in Portsmouth, Virginia (d. 1984)
Ladislav Holoubek, Slovak conductor (Košice Opera, 1966-81), and composer (Rodina), born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1994)
Liesbeth Askonas, concert agent, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1996)
Makarios III [Michail Moeskos], Greek Cypriot clergyman (Archbishop of Cyprus, 1950-77), and politician (1st President of Cyprus, 1966-74), born in Panayia, Paphos< British Cyprus (d. 1977)
"Cap'n Jack" McCarthy, American television broadcaster (NYC's St. Patrick Day Parade, 1949-89; kiddie show host (Popeye)), born in New York City (d. 1996)
Ivon De Bie, Belgian jazz pianist (Django Reinhardt), and bandleader, born in Molenbeek-Saint-Jean, Belgium (d. 1989)
Luis Mariano, Basque operetta singer, born in Irun, Spain (d. 1970)
Sid Gordon, American baseball player, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1975)
Denis Smallwood, British air chief marshal, born in Birmingham, England (d. 1997)

Frederick Sanger, British biochemist (worked out structure of insulin, developed Sanger sequencing to sequence complete genome, Nobel Prize 1958, 1980), born in Rendcomb, Gloucestershire, England (d. 2013)
John Bunting, Australian diplomat and senior civil servant, born in Ballarat, Victoria (d. 1995)
(Robert) "Baby Boy" Warren, American blues singer and guitarist, born in Lake Providence, Louisiana (d. 1977)
George Shearing, British-American blind jazz pianist, and composer ("Lullaby of Birdland"), born in London, England (d. 2011)
Rex Humbard, American television evangelist (Cathedral of Tomorrow), born in Little Rock, Arkansas (d. 2007)
Charlie Galbraith, British jazz trombonist, born in London (d. 1997)
Neville Brand, American soldier and actor (DOA, Love Me Tender), born in Griswold, Iowa (d. 1992)
Jimmy McCracklin [Walker], American R&B pianist, and composer ("The Walk"; "Tramp"), born in Elaine, Arkansas (d. 2012)
Louis Frémaux, French conductor (Monte-Carlo Philharmonic, 1956-65; City of Birmingham Symphony, 1969-78; Sydney Symphony, 1979-82), born in Aire-sur-la-Lys, France (d. 2017)
John Milne, British industrialist (Blue Circles cement), born in Manchester, England
Benny Bailey, American jazz trumpeter, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2005)
Joe Puma, American session and jazz guitarist (Herbie Mann; Artie Shaw's Gramercy Five; Chuck Wayne), born in the Bronx, New York City (d. 2000)
Augustyn Bloch, Polish composer, born in Grudziądz (d. 2006)
Michael Beavis, British Air Force officer (Commanding-in-Chief Allied Forces Central Europe), born in London (d. 2020)
Pat Harrington Jr, American actor (Danny Thomas Show, 1 Day at a Time), born in New York City (d. 2016)
Don Ho, Hawaiian-American pop and Hawaiian music singer ("Tiny Bubbles"; "Ain't No Big Thing!"), born in Honolulu, Territory of Hawaii (d. 2007)
Heino Jurisalu, Estonian composer (Kvintett puhkpillidele), born in Tartu, Estonia (d. 1991)
Wilmer Mizell, American politician (Rep-R-North Carolina 1969-75), and MLB baseball pitcher, 1952-62, 2X All-Star (St. Louis Cardinals and 2 other teams), born in Leakesville, Mississippi (d. 1999)
